text: I’ll inspect the Next.js entry points and existing request handling, then add logging at the narrowest shared boundary so every incoming request is covered without duplicating logs. I’ll verify the app’s available checks afterward.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc "pwd && rg --files -g '"'!*node_modules*'"' -g '"'!*.lock'"' | head -80 && printf '\\n--- package ---\\n' && (sed -n '1,220p' package.json 2>/dev/null || true) && printf '\\n--- next/config/middleware candidates ---\\n' && rg -n \"middleware|NextRequest|NextResponse|instrumentation|app/|pages/|request\" -g '"'!node_modules'"' -g '"'!*.lock'"' . | head -160"

text: This is a minimal App Router project with no existing middleware or server request wrapper. I’m adding a root request boundary that logs the method and URL before Next handles each request, with no matcher exclusions so asset and internal requests are included too.
The logger is now in place at [middleware.ts](/home/user/workspace/middleware.ts). I’m running the production build to catch any Next 16 convention or TypeScript issues before handing it back.

text: The build passed, but Next 16 reports that `middleware.ts` is deprecated in favor of `proxy.ts`. Since this app is already on Next 16, I’m updating to the current convention so the logging works without leaving a deprecation warning. The build also added Next’s standard generated type include to `tsconfig.json`.

text: The production server is running cleanly with the new `proxy.ts`; requests to both `/` and a missing route produced the expected `[request] GET ...` console lines. I’m doing one final file check now, including the build-generated TypeScript config change.

Added [proxy.ts](/home/user/workspace/proxy.ts), which logs every request as:

```text
[request] METHOD URL
```

Verified with a production build and requests to `/` and `/missing`; both were logged successfully.
